# Introduction to GitHub

Before next lab session, you should have the following completed

1.  R/Rstudio downloaded
2.  Git/GitHub downloaded
3.  GitHub shared with me
4.  R/Rstudio and Git/GitHub integrated
5.  GitHub repo organized

In order to complete the task "GitHub repo organized" and begin understanding how to use Git, complete this worksheet before next lab.

## Activity 1

1.  Edit your `README.md` *on your computer* in Rstudio by deleting all the text in the file and then replacing it with `# BISC 404L: Ecology and Biodiversity`. Be sure to save these changes, if saved correctly the filename in Rstudio should change from red to black.
2.  **Commit** your changes with a descriptive message. Do *not* push.

Go to your repo on your *browser*, what do you observe?

```{=plaintext}
```
Paste a screenshot of what you see (*to paste in screenshots, be sure to be viewing the `.qmd` in "Visual" mode*)

*Screenshot:*

3.  On your computer, push your changes

Go to your repo on your *browser*, what do you observe now?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

4.  Edit your `README.md` online in the browser by adding your name surrounded by one asterisk (\*) on each side on the line underneath "BISC 404L..."
5.  Commit your changes with a descriptive message

Go to the folder on your computer containing your repo, what do you observe?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

6.  Pull your changes to your local device

Go to the folder on your computer containing your repo, what do you observe now?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

7.  On your computer, create a new file called `test1.txt`
8.  Edit your `.gitignore` file to include the name `test1.txt` at the bottom of the `.gitignore` file. Save the `.gitignore` file.

Now what happens when you try to commit and push your changes your changes? What do you see in your browser versus on your computer?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

9.  On your computer, delete `test1.txt` from your `.gitignore` file. Save your `.gitignore` file. Now what happens when you try to commit and push on your computer? What do you see in your browser versus on your computer?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

10. For question 7-9, what did you learn about how the `.gitignore` file works?

```{=plaintext}
```
11. On your computer create a new, empty folder in your repo called "test_folder".

What do you observe on your browser when you try to commit and push?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

12. Now add a file called `test2.txt` inside your folder "test_folder"

Now what do you observe on your browser when you try to commit and push? Why did it change?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

13. On your browser, edit the `test2.txt` by writing the word "green" in it

14. Commit. Do *not* push.

15. On your desktop, edit the `test2.txt` by writing the word "blue" in it

16. Commit

Now what do you observe when you try to push from your computer?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

Why is that?

```{=plaintext}
```
17. On your computer, open "test2.txt" in Rstudio. You should see that the file has changed.

What do you think these new symbols mean?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

18. Delete all the text in the file except for "green", save, and commit

Now what do you observe when you try to pull from your browser?

```{=plaintext}
```
*Screenshot:*

19. Why do you think this happened?

```{=plaintext}
```
## Activity 2

Take time to better organize the folder/file structure of your repo

-   Remember, here is a template format you can use:

    -   ![](../images/clipboard-3401715733.png){width="264"}

    -   [Template Repository](https://github.com/CharlesLehnen/standard_repository_template)

-   You can also follow a template like this for coursework:

    -   ![](../images/clipboard-828242083.png){width="162"}

-   Or you can make whatever folder structure makes sense to you, as long as it is intentional and logical

::: callout-warning
## GitHub Warnings!

-   **Note:** GitHub does *not* like empty folders

-   GitHub does not do well with large files. Best to add them to `.gitignore` and back them up separately (Google Drive, OneDrive, etc.)
:::

## Activity 3

Underneath the course title and your name, on your computer add whatever descriptive text you would like to your `README.md` file. You could add a description of your folder structure, you could start a list talking about which topics we covered in which labs, you could start a documents folder and download the course syllabus to it, etc.

For inspiration, you could look at [this README.](https://github.com/CharlesLehnen/standard_repository_template/blob/main/README.md)

Add some style to your README following this guide:

![](images/clipboard-1482354080.png)\
[*Source*](https://lukegearing.blot.im/using-markdown-and-pandoc-to-make-rpg-documents-for-free)

*To add styles using this guide, be sure to be viewing this `.qmd` in "Source" mode. You can toggle to "Visual" mode to double check that style changes are being made as you intend.*

![](images/clipboard-561899689.png)

Next lab session, I will identify some repos that I would like to showcase to the rest of the students, so think about your organization logically and creatively. There are no right or wrong ways to organize, just so it makes sense to you and you stay consistent!
